title: Peace Gets a Time-Out Extension: Israel and Lebanon Agree to 3 More Weeks of Ceasefire
summary:
Imagine you're in a huge argument with your neighbor. Then someone says, "Hey, let's all take a break for a few weeks." That's exactly what happened between Israel and Lebanon. They agreed to a ceasefire (a promise to stop fighting) for three more weeks. President Trump helped them talk at the White House on Thursday.

The first break was only 10 days long. Now they've added 21 more days. The ambassadors from both countries shook hands and said "thank you" to the U.S. for helping.

But here's the tricky part: Not everyone is happy. A group called Hezbollah (say: HEZ-buh-lah) said it won't follow the agreement. And a journalist named Amal Khalil was killed by an Israeli strike while reporting in Lebanon. That made a lot of people really sad and angry.

Still, the leaders hope this extra time will help them talk about bigger things — like stopping attacks for good, getting soldiers to go home, and rebuilding houses that were destroyed. About 2,300 people have died in this war, and over 1 million had to leave their homes. That's like the whole city of Dallas being empty.

So will this time-out turn into real peace? We'll have to wait and see.
why_it_matters: Even when countries are far away, peace agreements affect everyone because wars hurt families and force people to leave their homes.

keywords.explanation: a militant group in Lebanon that rejects the ceasefire
a person who reports the news, like Amal Khalil
the U.S. president's home and office, where talks happened
a conflict between groups that has killed many people
a time when fighting stops and people try to get along

options: She was killed by an Israeli strike

background_read: Israel and Lebanon have had many conflicts over the years, especially along their shared border.
Hezbollah is a powerful group in Lebanon that does not always agree with the Lebanese government.
A ceasefire is a promise to stop fighting so that leaders can try to make a lasting peace.
Article_Structure: WHO: Israel and Lebanon, with help from U.S. President Trump
WHAT: They agreed to extend a ceasefire by three weeks
WHERE: The White House in Washington, D.C.
WHY: To allow time for talks about stopping attacks, withdrawing soldiers, and rebuilding homes

perspectives.description: The ceasefire extension gives hope that more talks can lead to real peace and safety.
Hezbollah rejecting the deal and the killing of a journalist show that peace is still fragile.
A ceasefire is a small step, but many difficult issues remain.
